skill.name: yescan-transoffice-universal
skill.description: Convert images, screenshots, and scanned documents into editable Word, Excel, or PDF files. Preserves complex layouts including tables, contracts, and mixed text-image content.
skill.summary: Image-to-Office document conversion with layout preservation, powered by Quark Scanner.
skill.author: yescan-ai

security_audit.summary: The skill is a legitimate document conversion tool that makes network calls to an OCR API and reads API keys from environment variables. Most static findings are false positives caused by the scanner detecting backtick characters in Markdown documentation, file path references, and Chinese-language content. The actual code in scripts/common/ uses standard patterns for file processing and HTTP requests to the OCR service. No malicious intent was found.

high_findings.title: Environment variable access for API credentials
high_findings.description: scripts/common/ocr_client.py reads multiple environment variables (lines 65-70, 95, 100, 105) likely containing API keys for the OCR service. This is expected for a cloud-based OCR integration but represents credential access. The variables appear to be API keys for the Quark Scanner service.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern clearly shows os.getenv() calls for API credentials, consistent with legitimate OCR service authentication. No evidence of credential exfiltration to unauthorized endpoints.

high_findings.title: Outbound HTTP requests to OCR service
high_findings.description: scripts/common/ocr_client.py makes HTTP requests to an external OCR API endpoint (lines 109, 148, 183, 202, 224, 253). Images are uploaded to the Quark Scanner service for processing. This is the core functionality of the skill but constitutes network egress of user data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple HTTP client calls in ocr_client.py confirm the skill sends images to an external API for OCR processing. This is the intended functionality.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic code patterns in __init__.py
medium_findings.description: scripts/common/__init__.py (lines 10-11) contains patterns flagged as eval(), exec(), and __import__(). On review, these are likely standard library imports and string operations, not actual dynamic code execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static scanner flagged these patterns but in context of an __init__.py file, they are most likely import statements or module-level configuration, not dangerous eval/exec calls.

medium_findings.title: Base64 decode operations in file_saver.py
medium_findings.description: scripts/common/file_saver.py uses base64 decoding (lines 19, 151, 185, 224). This is likely for handling base64-encoded image data from the OCR API response, not for obfuscation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Base64 in file_saver.py is contextually appropriate for processing API response data containing encoded file content.

medium_findings.title: Temporary file and directory access
medium_findings.description: The skill creates and accesses temporary files and directories (scripts/common/file_saver.py lines 8, 25, 67) for intermediate processing of converted documents. Standard pattern for file processing pipelines.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Temp directory usage is standard for document processing pipelines and poses no additional risk beyond normal file I/O.

low_findings.title: Static scanner false positives in Markdown documentation
low_findings.description: The vast majority of 'external_commands', 'weak crypto', 'Windows SAM', and 'hidden file' findings are false positives caused by the static scanner matching backtick characters in Markdown code blocks, Chinese-language text, and file path examples in documentation files (README.md, SKILL.md, examples/*.md, references/*.md).
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns appear in Markdown documentation files describing usage, not in executable code. Backticks, hash algorithms, and file paths in docs are not security threats.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network data egress to OCR API
dangerous_patterns.description: User images are sent to an external OCR service (Quark Scanner) for processing. This is the core functionality but users should be aware their images leave their machine.

content.user_title: Convert Images to Editable Office Documents
content.value_statement: Converting scanned documents and images into editable Word, Excel, or PDF files is tedious and error-prone. This skill automates the process by sending images to an OCR service and generating formatted, editable documents with preserved layout.
actual_capabilities: Converts images, screenshots, and scanned documents to editable Word (.docx) files
Converts table-heavy images to Excel (.xlsx) spreadsheets with preserved cell structure
Generates PDF files from images with layout preservation
Handles complex layouts including tables, contracts, and mixed text-image content
Supports Chinese and English document content with Quark Scanner OCR engine
limitations: Requires an API key for the Quark Scanner OCR service to function
Sends user images to an external cloud service for processing (privacy consideration)
OCR accuracy depends on image quality — handwritten or low-resolution scans may produce errors
Cannot extract plain text only — always generates formatted document output

use_cases.title: Digitize paper contracts
use_cases.description: Convert scanned contract images into editable Word documents while preserving formatting and layout structure.

use_cases.title: Extract data from table screenshots
use_cases.description: Transform table images and screenshots into Excel spreadsheets with proper cell alignment and data structure.

use_cases.title: Convert lecture slides to PDFs
use_cases.description: Turn presentation screenshots and handwritten notes into searchable PDF documents for study and reference.

prompt_templates.title: Basic image to Word conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the image at /path/to/scan.jpg to an editable Word document.
prompt_templates.scenario: Simple conversion of a single image to .docx format

prompt_templates.title: Table image to Excel
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this table screenshot to an Excel file, preserving the row and column structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Extracting tabular data from an image into a spreadsheet

prompt_templates.title: Batch convert multiple images
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert all images in /path/to/scans/ to PDF format and save them in /path/to/output/.
prompt_templates.scenario: Processing multiple scanned documents at once

prompt_templates.title: Complex layout with mixed content
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this scanned contract image to Word format, preserving the original layout, headers, and table structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Handling documents with complex formatting requirements

output_examples.input: Convert this table screenshot to Excel

best_practices: Ensure source images are high resolution (300 DPI or higher) for best OCR accuracy
Configure the QUARK_API_KEY environment variable before first use
Review the generated document for accuracy before sharing, especially for critical documents
Use the appropriate output format: Word for editable text, Excel for tables, PDF for archival
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for extracting plain text — use a dedicated OCR text extraction tool instead
Do not process images containing sensitive personal data without reviewing the privacy implications of cloud OCR
Do not expect perfect accuracy on handwritten content or severely degraded scans

faq.question: What image formats are supported?
faq.answer: The skill supports common image formats including PNG, JPG, JPEG, BMP, and TIFF. PDF input files are also accepted for conversion to editable formats.

faq.question: Is an API key required?
faq.answer: Yes, you need a Quark Scanner API key configured as the QUARK_API_KEY environment variable. The skill uses this service for OCR processing.

faq.question: Are my images sent to a cloud service?
faq.answer: Yes, images are uploaded to the Quark Scanner OCR service for text recognition and layout analysis. Review the privacy documentation if you handle sensitive data.

faq.question: How accurate is the OCR?
faq.answer: Accuracy depends on image quality. Clean, high-resolution scans of printed text typically achieve 95%+ accuracy. Handwritten text and low-quality scans may have lower accuracy.

faq.question: Can it handle Chinese documents?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill supports both Chinese (Simplified and Traditional) and English documents with proper layout preservation.

faq.question: What is the difference between Word, Excel, and PDF output?
faq.answer: Word (.docx) is best for editable text documents. Excel (.xlsx) is best for tabular data. PDF is best for archival and sharing while preserving the original layout exactly.
